title-group.article-title: Major Depressive Disorder in Long COVID and Exposure to Pro‐Inflammatory Cytokines During Infection by SARS‐CoV‐2
title-group.alt-title: Major Depressive Disorder in Long COVID

aff.named-content: Centro de Investigaciones en Ciencias de la Vida
aff.institution: Center for Clinical and Translational Research
Center for Clinical and Translational Research

aff.named-content: Department of Psychiatry and Mental Health

aff.institution: Zerenia Clinic. Consultation ‐ Liaison Psychiatric Unit
Research and Innovation Center, and Universidad de la Costa

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2025 The Author(s). Psychiatric Research and Clinical Practice published by Wiley Periodicals LLC on behalf of American Psychiatric Association.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/ License, which permits use, distribution and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited and is not used for commercial purposes.

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) is common in long COVID syndrome; however, the neurobiological mechanisms are unclear. An immune activation response has been associated with COVID‐19 severity as well as in MDD. We hypothesize that high levels of pro‐inflammatory cytokines during SARS‐CoV‐2 infection may be associated with new‐onset MDD and severe outcomes such as treatment‐resistant depression (TRD) and risk of suicide ideation and behavior (SI/SB).

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A case‐control nested to a cohort study was carried out on a total of 678 COVID‐19 survivors (MDD = 184 vs. non‐MDD = 494). A pro‐inflammatory panel of serum levels of cytokines (IL‐1β, IL‐4, IL‐6, IL‐8, IL‐13, IL‐17α, TNF‐α) was evaluated during COVID‐19 hospitalization and severe outcomes such as TRD and SI/SB were assessed in individuals with new onset of MDD after hospital discharge compared to non‐MDD COVID‐19 survivors.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: High levels of pro‐inflammatory cytokines during SARS‐CoV‐2 infection were identified in MDD participants compared to non‐MDD subgroups during long COVID. These differences were sustained also for TRD and SI/SB severity outcomes. There is a mild association of high levels of pro‐inflammatory cytokines and MDD, TRD, and SI/SB.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: High levels of pro‐inflammatory cytokines during severe or critical COVID‐19 exposure may explain long COVID associated MDD and thus severe outcomes such as TRD and SI/SB.

sec.title: Relevance to clinical practice
sec.p: Identifying elevated pro‐inflammatory cytokines during COVID‐19 as a risk factor for MDD and severe outcomes underscores the need for early screening and targeted treatments in long COVID. Monitoring cytokine levels may help clinicians predict and manage TRD and SI/SB in this population, improving long‐term psychiatric outcomes.

list-item.p: High levels of pro‐inflammatory cytokines during SARS‐CoV‐2 infection were identified in Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) participants compared to non‐MDD subgroups during long COVID.
There is a mild association of high levels of pro‐inflammatory cytokines and MDD, treatment‐resistant depression (TRD), and SI/SB.
High levels of pro‐inflammatory cytokines during severe or critical COVID‐19 exposure may explain long COVID associated MDD and thus severe outcomes such as TRD and SI/SB.

award-group.funding-source: The Center for Clinical and Translational Research

fn.p: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of clinicians and assistant researchers in the setting for data collection. Also, we thank patients and their families for the support during the follow‐up. This study was supported by The CCTR at Universidad Simón Bolivar, Universidad El Bosque, Clinica La Misericordia Internacional, and Clinica Bonnadona.

Hernan F. Guillen‐Burgos: he has received research grant support from the Ministry of Science, Technology, and Innovation (MinCiencias) in Colombia, UKRI in the United Kingdom; and speaker fees from Roche, Pfizer, Abbott, GSK, and Synergy R&D.

Juan F. Gálvez‐Flórez: The author is a full‐time employee for Zerenia Clinics Bogotá, Colombia. He also serves as a scientific advisor for mental health programs for Salud Bosque IPS Bogotá, Colombia.

Roger S. McIntyre: he has received research grant support from CIHR/GACD/National Natural Science Foundation of China and the Milken Institute; and speaker/consultation fees from Lundbeck, Janssen, Alkermes, Neumora Therapeutics, Boehringer Ingelheim, Sage, Biogen, Mitsubishi Tanabe, Purdue, Pfizer, Otsuka, Takeda, Neurocrine, Neurawell, Sunovion, Bausch Health, Axsome, Novo Nordisk, Kris, Sanofi, Eisai, Intra‐Cellular, NewBridge Pharmaceuticals, Viatris, Abbvie, and Atai Life Sciences. RM is a CEO of Braxia Scientific Corp.
Sergio Moreno‐López, Mariana Ramirez‐Posada, Sabrina Wong, Angela T. H. Kwan, and Juan‐Manuel Anaya: The authors declare no conflict of interest to report.

body.p: Depression is a common sequelae in long COVID symptomatology (1). Around 20‐40% of COVID‐19 survivors report depressive symptoms after SARS‐CoV‐2 infection along with fatigue, cognitive impairment, unspecified anxiety symptoms, insomnia, and other physical and neuropsychiatric outcomes (2). Moreover, the development of MDD has been reported after SARS‐CoV‐2 and considered as a novel long COVID neuropsychiatric manifestation (1, 3). However, it is unclear which are the neurobiological mechanisms underlying this association in COVID‐19 survivors.

Over the past decades, several biological pathways have been elucidated to contribute to MDD neurobiology (4). In this sense, inflammatory and immune responses have a pivotal role on the onset of MDD according to animal and human studies (5, 6). At this time, COVID‐19 is considered a multisystemic syndrome mediated by a significant pro‐inflammatory response accompanied by a higher cytokines release syndrome in severe, recurrent or prolonged SARS‐CoV‐2 infections (7, 8). In fact, high levels of several cytokines as a consequence of cellular immune response have been observed in COVID‐19 participants with different concentrations according to severity of illness (9). Nevertheless, it is not known whether elevated levels of active immune response during SARS‐CoV‐2 infection are related to MDD onset during the post‐infection period in COVID survivors. Consequently, there is an urgent need to understand physio‐pathological mechanisms in long COVID neuropsychiatric disorders.

Severe outcomes in MDD such as TRD and suicidal ideation/suicidal behavior (SI/SB) have been associated with neuroinflammation (10, 11). Previous studies in the pre‐pandemic era reported that higher baseline IL‐6 levels predicted better response to medication provided with anti‐inflammatory properties such as ketamine and infliximab compared to a poor response to antidepressants in MDD (10). Furthermore, multiple studies have suggested that patients who experience suicidality may experience higher inflammation (11). In other words, infection causes inflammation, which then triggers complex neuroinflammatory processes described above, leading to MDD, and thus predicting TRD and SI/SB during the course of illness.

Therefore, we hypothesized that individuals who suffered severe or critical COVID‐19 and developed new onset MDD after being discharged may have had an elevated proinflammatory cytokine profile while admitted to hospital wards and the intensive care units (ICU). We also considered exploring if a high proinflammatory cytokine profile may be associated with TRD and SI/SB severity outcomes during the course of MDD illness in the first year after SARS‐CoV‐2 infection. To elucidate both hypotheses associated with novel long COVID syndromes, we conducted a nested case‐control study within a cohort of COVID‐19 survivors.

sec.title: Study Design
sec.p: A case‐control nested cohort study matched by ages of participants with MDD (n = 184) and non‐MDD (n = 494) with severe or critical COVID‐19 exposure. Study setting was located in the Center for Clinical and Translational Research (CCTR) in Barranquilla, Colombia. All subjects were hospitalized during COVID‐19 in two teaching hospitals at Barranquilla, Colombia (Clinica La Misericordia Internacional and Clinica Bonnadona) and were referred to mental health assessments and follow‐up in a large cohort of COVID‐19 survivors at the CCTR. A sample size calculation was based on a prevalence of depression in Long COVID of 23% (12), a significance level of 95%, a power of 90%, assuming an Odds Ratio of 2, a case‐control ratio of 1:2, assuming a loss during follow‐up of 10%, the minimum sample size was 173 cases and 346 controls.

sec.title: Participants
sec.p: Individuals who had COVID‐19 severe or critical according to national COVID‐19 guidelines were included in the study (13). Two subgroups were selected. Participants between 18 and 64 years old with new diagnosis of MDD according to DSM‐5‐TR criteria after hospital discharge for COVID‐19, matched by age with another group of COVID ‐ 19 survivors without MDD. All participants were assessed by trained psychiatrists and clinicians using the Structured Clinical Interview for DMS‐5 Clinician Version (SCID‐5‐CV). Collection data was approved by the ethics committee from Universidad Simon Bolivar (Approval number: CEI‐USB‐CE‐0324‐00‐00). All individuals accepted to participate and provide written and informed consent including approval for publication.

sec.title: Exposure
sec.p: The selected exposure was having severe or critical COVID‐19 according to national guidelines and expert consensus, and a complete pro‐inflammatory protein profile (IL‐1b, IL‐4, IL‐6, IL‐8, IL‐13, IL‐17a, TNF‐a) during hospitalization. Severe COVID‐19 was defined as adults with clinical signs of pneumonia (fever, cough, dyspnea, tachypnea) plus one of the following: respiratory rate >30 breaths/min; severe respiratory distress; or SpO2 <90% in room air. Chest imaging (X‐ray, CT scan, ultrasound) was considered as an additional diagnostic tool to identify or exclude other associated pulmonary complications. Furthermore, critical COVID‐19 was defined as an adult with signs of acute respiratory distress syndrome, sepsis, septic shock, or acute thrombosis. At the time, clinical definitions of severity were used according to the national COVID‐19 guidelines provided by Colombian Association of Infectiology (13).

sec.title: Pro‐Inflammatory Protein Profile
sec.p: Upon admission to the ICU blood samples were collected to measure routinary laboratory test and cytokines profile levels. Serum was obtained after centrifugation for 10 min at 2000 rpm and each serum sample was stored at −80°C until processing. A Human ProcartaPlex TM Multiplex Immunoassay Mix & Match of 7 – Plex based on magnetic beads was selected to detect serum proteins (Invitrogen, Whatman, Massachusetts, United States). These analytes included: IL‐1beta, IL‐4, IL‐6, IL‐8, IL‐13, IL‐17 alpha, TNF‐alpha. Undiluted samples were processed following the manufacturer instructions. Then, the analytes were analyzed using the Luminex® 100/200TM (ThermoFisher Scientific, Luminex Corporation 12212 Technology Blvd. Austin, Texas 78727). All samples and standards were measured in duplicate. Primary data were analyzed using Xponet® Software (Luminex, Austin, Texas, United States).

sec.title: Treatment‐Resistant Depression
sec.p: Treatment‐resistant depression was defined as failure to respond to two or more antidepressant regimens despite adequate dose, duration, and adherence to treatment (14). The TRD was defined by a psychiatrist and reported in the electronic health records.

sec.title: Suicide Ideation and Behavior
sec.p: Suicide ideation and behavior was measured through Columbia‐Suicide Severity Rating Scale (C‐SSRS). The C‐SSRS is a tool that used 4 constructs (severity ideation, intensity ideation, behavior, and lethality) (15). Suicidal ideation was outlined as any self‐harming or death‐wishing thoughts because both have been linked to an increased risk of suicide behavior (16, 17). Any suicide attempt (including interrupted or abandoned attempts) or preparations for an attempt reported on the C‐SSRS were considered suicidal behavior (18). In comparison to expert consensus ratings for suicidal conduct, the C‐SSRS has been demonstrated to have extremely high sensitivity and specificity as well as being sensitive to change over time (15). This scale has been used previously in bipolar disorder (BD) participants (19).

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: Analyses were performed in Stata v18 SE. Categorical variables were presented as absolute or relative values, and continuous variables were presented as mean with the corresponding standard deviation. A t‐test was used to compare hypothesis testing for continuous variables. Logistic regression analysis was used to detect association between clinical and biological markers (independent variables) during exposure, and TRD and suicide ideation/behavior (dependent variables) as outcomes. The regression models were adjusted by social and clinical factors (age, gender, body mass index, length hospital stay, MoCA mean score, MADRS mean score, and mechanical ventilation). The performance of the models was compared using the Akaike criteria (AIC) and the Bayesian information criteria. Model assumptions were validated through a linearity test, the Hosmer–Lemeshow test, an estimation of deviance residuals and Cook's values, and a comparison between the crude and the adjusted models. Plots were generated with ggplot2 packages in R free software. All analyses had a 95% confidence interval, alpha error 0.05, and were two‐tailed.

sec.title: RESULTS
sec.p: A total of 678 participants matched by age were considered COVID survivors (MDD = 184 vs. non‐MDD = 494) and were included. With regards to demographics in the MDD group, the mean age was 39.8 (SD = 13.1), 56.8% were men, 55.2% were married, 39.3% graduated from high school, 77.1% were Hispanic, and 55.7% lived through scarcity associated with low socioeconomic status. Clinical characteristics reported in the MDD group were a mean of 18.9 (SD = 7.3) days of hospital stay during COVID‐19 hospitalization, a BMI mean of 32.6 (SD = 5.1), MoCA mean was 26.9 (SD = 2.3), anxiety symptoms in 62.8%, insomnia symptoms in 78.7%, TRD in 40%, and suicide ideation/behavior in 28% (Table 1). Additionally, acute COVID‐19 and post‐COVID‐19 symptoms were evaluated and showed in the Figure 1.

fn.label: a
fn.p: BMI, Body Mass Index; MDD, Major Depressive Disorder; MoCA, Montreal Cognitive Assessment; SI/SB, Suicide Ideation/Behavior; TRD, Treatment‐Resistant Depression.

sec.title: Differences in Cytokines Profiles Between Major Depressive Disorder Versus Non‐MDD Exposure to COVID‐19 Severe and Critical
sec.p: There are higher levels of cytokines (IL‐1beta, IL‐4, IL‐6, IL‐8, IL‐13, IL‐17 alpha, TNF‐alpha) in participants with MDD than non‐MDD with previous exposure to severe COVID‐19 (t = 16.0, df = 373, p < 0.001; t = 7.7, df = 373, p < 0.001; t = 3.9, df = 373, p < 0.001; t = 3.5, df = 373, p < 0.001; t = 10.1, df = 373, p < 0.001; t = 7.6, df = 373, p < 0.001; t = 2.03, df = 373, p = 0.042, respectively) (Figure 2A).

caption.p: A. Cytokines profiles of Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) versus non‐MDD with previous severe COVID‐19 (n = 375). B. Cytokines profiles in participants with treatment‐resistant‐depression (TRD) and previous severe COVID‐19 (n = 375). C. Cytokines profiles in SI/SB with previous severe COVID‐19 (n = 375).

sec.p: Also, for critical COVID‐19 survivors higher levels of cytokines were identified in the group of MDD than in non‐MDD (t = 8.0, df = 301, p < 0.001; t = 7.7, df = 301, p < 0.001; t = 7.5, df = 301, p < 0.001; t = 7.4, df = 301, p < 0.001; t = 8.1, df = 301, p < 0.001; t = 7.6, df = 301, p < 0.001; t = 7.3, df = 301, p < 0.001, respectively) (Table 2).

caption.p: Cytokines profile in Major Depressive Disorder (MDD), treatment‐resistant depression (TRD), SI/SB with critical COVID‐19 exposure (n = 303). a

fn.label: a
fn.p: MDD, Major Depressive Disorder; SI/SB, Suicide Ideation/Suicide Behavior; TRD, Treatment‐Resistant‐Depression.

sec.title: Differences in Cytokines Profiles Between treatment‐resistant depression Versus Non‐TRD Exposure to COVID‐19 Severe and Critical
sec.p: High levels of cytokines (IL‐1beta, IL‐4, IL‐6, IL‐8, IL‐13, IL‐17 alpha, TNF‐alpha) were identified in the TRD group compared to non‐TRD with severe COVID‐19 exposure (t = −8.1, df = 373, p < 0.001; t = −5.2, df = 373, p < 0.001; t = −5.0, df = 373, p < 0.001; t = −10.2, df = 373, p < 0.001; t = −6.4, df = 373, p < 0.001; t = −3.8, df = 373, p < 0.001; t = −2.6, df = 373, p = 0.042, respectively) (Figure 2B).

Besides, for critical COVID‐19 survivors elevated levels of concentrations in the cytokines profile were identified for TRD than non‐TRD group (t = −14.2, df = 301, p < 0.001; t = −11.9, df = 301, p < 0.001; t = −11.5, df = 301, p < 0.001; t = −11.1, df = 301, p < 0.001; t = −13.2, df = 301, p < 0.001; t = −11.8, df = 301, p < 0.001; t = −10.3, df = 301, p < 0.001, respectively) (Table 2).

sec.title: Differences in Cytokines Profiles Between SI/SB Versus Non‐SI/SB Exposure to COVID‐19 Severe and Critical
sec.p: High levels of cytokines (IL‐1beta, IL‐4, IL‐6, IL‐8, IL‐13, IL‐17 alpha, TNF‐alpha) were identified in the SI/SB group compared to non‐SI/SB with severe COVID‐19 exposure (t = −5.30, df = 373, p < 0.001; t = −1.7, df = 373, p = 0.091; t = −3.6, df = 373, p < 0.001; t = −3.5, df = 373, p < 0.001; t = −4.5, df = 373, p < 0.001; t = −3.0, df = 373, p = 0.003; t = −1.5, df = 373, p = 0.121, respectively) (Figure 2C).

Also, for critical COVID‐19 survivors elevated levels of concentrations in the cytokines profile were identified for SI/SB than for the non‐SI/SB group (t = −8.6, df = 301, p < 0.001; t = −8.2, df = 301, p < 0.001; t = −7.9, df = 301, p < 0.001; t = −8.0, df = 301, p < 0.001; t = −8.8, df = 301, p < 0.001; t = −8.2, df = 301, p < 0.001; t = −7.7, df = 301, p < 0.001, respectively) (Table 2).

sec.title: Association Between Pro‐Inflammatory Cytokines and Major Depressive Disorder, Treatment‐Resistant Depression and SI/SB
sec.p: IL‐1b, IL‐4, IL‐6, IL‐8, IL‐13, and IL‐17 alpha during severe and critical COVID‐19 exposure were associated with new onset MDD and TRD. TNF‐alpha was only associated with TRD in severe and critical COVID‐19 survivors. IL‐1b, IL‐6, and IL‐13 were associated during severe COVID‐19 exposure with SI/SB. In critical COVID‐19 exposure the interleukins IL‐1b, IL‐6, IL‐8, and IL‐13 were positive associated with SI/SB during the long COVID syndrome (Table 3).

caption.p: Cytokines as risk factors with Major Depressive Disorder (MDD), treatment‐resistant depression (TRD) and SI/SB in Long COVID syndrome. a

fn.label: a
fn.p: Abbreviations: IL, Interleukin; MDD, Major Depressive Disorder; OR, Odds Ratio; SI/SB, Suicide Ideation/Suicide Behavior; TNF, Tumoral Necrosis Factor; TRD, Treatment‐Resistant Depression.

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: To our knowledge, this is the first analytical study evaluating pro‐inflammatory profiles during COVID‐19 hospitalization in patients with long COVID and new onset MDD after hospital discharge. Our results elucidate that there are differences between cytokines profiles for MDD, TRD, and SI/SB in COVID survivors with a severe or critical COVID‐19 exposure. Likewise, our results also highlight an association of higher levels of cytokines during severe and critical COVID‐19 exposure with mental health status during long COVID as MDD, TRD, and SI/SB.

Previous studies have identified that high levels of cytokines during COVID‐19 may predicts survival and mortality (20). Particularly, in Long COVID participants have reported elevated IL‐1b, IL‐6, and TNF‐alpha (21, 22, 23). Beforehand, this pattern of elevated proinflammatory proteins has been identified also in MDD, TRD, SI/SB (5, 10, 11). In this sense, our findings highlight new evidence regarding the differences in the concentration of pro‐inflammatory analytes with higher levels in groups of COVID survivors with MDD, TRD and SI/SB compared to non‐MDD, non‐TRD, and non‐SI/SB COVID survivors. These novel results enable us to support the role of a pro‐inflammatory state during SARS‐CoV‐2 infection. Results also suggest that proinflammatory pathways may be associated with both onset and severity of MDD.

Neuropsychiatric signs and symptoms have been described and associated during long COVID syndromes (1, 3). Moreover, several clinical variables of COVID infection in survivors have been associated with new onset of established psychiatric disorders such as MDD. For example, length of hospital stay, mechanical ventilation, or cytokines release syndrome have been associated with MDD aside from a concurrent SARS‐CoV‐2 infection (24, 25, 26). Nevertheless, our results showed that cytokines are associated with MDD, TRD, and SI/SB in COVID survivors who suffered severe and/or critical COVID‐19 infections. Differences remained after controlling specific confounders. Hence, our results support the important role of previous immune activation during SARS‐CoV‐2 infection and its associations with new‐onset of MDD and severity of illness in participants with recognized long COVID syndrome.

Treatment‐resistant depression is present up to 55% of patients prescribed antidepressants for MDD (31, 32, 33). Different factors have been associated with TRD onset (14). With regards to inflammatory response or immune activation and TRD, high levels of IL‐6 have been associated with worse response to antidepressants and better response to novel anti‐inflammatory agents such as ketamine and infliximab (10). Although few studies are available reporting cytokines levels in TRD, these targets may hold a promising biological marker for treatment response (10, 27). We reported a prevalence of TRD of 40% associated with high levels of the entire proinflammatory cytokine panel assessed including IL‐6. At present, we may argue that high levels of cytokines reported during hospitalization by COVID‐19 survivors may predispose this subgroup of participants for further brain‐damage and induce cellular stress, degeneration, synaptic alteration, astrogliosis, inflammation, and finally loss of neural function (28). Thus, proinflammatory patterns in COVID survivors may be similar to those described elsewhere for other neuropsychiatric disorders in which neuroinflammatory processes are overtly discussed such as BD, Schizophrenia (SCZ) and Alzheimer Disease among others (29, 30, 31).

In the same line, a recent study showed that high levels of IL‐6 may contribute to abnormalities in corticostriatal connectivity in participants with depression and impact the reward circuitry promoting reducing motivation and anhedonic behavior (32). TRD patients also show reduced motivation and anhedonia associated with decreased resting‐state functional connectivity in corticostriatal circuitry with higher levels of IL‐6 (33). Therefore, immune activation during severe infections such as SARS‐CoV‐2 via corticostriatal dysfunction may explain TRD in long COVID participants.

There is evidence that immunomodulation with infliximab only reduces depression‐related outcomes in TRD participants with high baseline levels of pro‐inflammatory peripheral molecules such as high hs‐CRP (34, 35). In that sense, infliximab may be useful for TRD in long COVID participants. However, the aforementioned hypothesis has not been tested in TRD and long COVID syndrome. According to a recent study, infliximab, when used in conjunction with tocilizumab, improves severity outcomes in COVID‐19 patients and has been suggested to be a promising treatment for those with moderate to severe COVID‐19 (36). One can argue that post discharge MDD is also another severity marker of COVID illness. Hence, it is possible that individuals with acute‐severe COVID‐19 who received infliximab treatment may delay or prevent the onset of MDD during long‐term COVID, TRD and thus long COVID syndrome in itself.

Suicidality and inflammatory cytokines, specifically IL‐1b and IL‐6, have been strongly associated in the past (37, 38). A sustained immune activation response may alter several neurobiological downstreams, such as the metabolism of monoamines, the kynurenine pathway's increased metabolism of tryptophan, and the dysregulation of the hypothalamic‐pituitary axis which raises the risk of suicidal behavior (11). There are few studies reporting suicide risk during long COVID (39, 40). We provide a potential association between suicide ideation or behavior during long COVID and elevated levels of cytokines (IL‐1b, IL‐6, IL‐8, and IL‐13) during previous SARS‐CoV‐2 infection. However, it is crucial to understand that suicidality is not solely based on an immune response. There are other factors that can contribute to suicide risk, such as genetics, early life adversities, and behavioral traits such as impulsivity and aggressive behavior (11).

In the post‐COVID‐19 era, a growing body of evidence suggests that SARS‐CoV‐2 infection triggers complex immune responses, marked by the production of effector molecules such as pro‐inflammatory cytokines and chemokines. This heightened immune activity is associated with disruptions in the blood‐brain barrier (BBB), a critical structure responsible for maintaining CNS homeostasis. The compromise of the BBB can result in leukocyte infiltration, potentially leading to inflammation within the brain, microhemorrhages, and subsequent neuronal damage (28).

Recent findings have elucidated the significant role of BBB disruption and sustained systemic inflammation in the pathophysiology of cognitive impairment associated with long COVID. Individuals with cognitive deficits following SARS‐CoV‐2 infection exhibited persistent systemic inflammation and compromised BBB integrity, which facilitated the infiltration of inflammatory molecules and immune cells into the central nervous system. Such neuroinflammatory processes were linked to the observed cognitive dysfunction, suggesting that the breakdown of the BBB may act as a conduit for peripheral immune activation to impact brain function adversely (41).

The development of neuropsychiatric disorders such as MDD after an acute‐severe COVID infection or associated with persistent long COVID syndrome requires moving forward in research in order to clarify the specific role of immune activation during COVID‐19 and persistent immune response in long COVID patients. Perhaps, precision psychiatry may enable us to identify new biological markers in this plausible physio‐pathological pathway that leads to severe depressive illness, such as TRD and SI/SB. Further down the line, it may help develop novel therapeutic targets for the spectrum of participants suffering from both long COVID and TRD.

This study has several strengths: 1) a large cohort of COVID‐19 survivors with new onset MDD, 2) a matched age control group for comparison, 3) a high sensitivity panel for detecting concentration levels of analytes regarding immune activation response during SARS‐CoV‐2 infection, 4) a validated widely used scale for detecting suicide behavior, and 5) generalized regression models controlling possible confounders. The limitations are related to: 1) the non‐inclusion of COVID‐19 survivors with mild or moderate disease, 2) the absence of a pro‐inflammatory panel evaluation in patients with mild or moderate COVID‐19 during their hospital stay and 3) serum cytokine levels may not fully capture CNS‐specific inflammation, 4) counterfactuals and other variables not included in the model as possible causes (multicausality) of MDD, and 5) the differences in demographic and clinical characteristics, such as BMI, MoCA and MADRS mean score may contribute to the variability in cytokine levels and the associated mental health outcomes. While statistical methods were employed to adjust for known confounders, residual confounding could still be present. This variability may affect the generalizability of our results and the strength of the associations drawn between systemic inflammation and MDD outcomes. Future studies with more homogenous or balanced cohorts are warranted to validate these findings and mitigate potential biases inherent in case‐control comparisons.

sec.title: CONCLUSIONS
sec.p: This study demonstrates that exposure to high levels of pro‐inflammatory cytokines is associated with the de novo appearance of MDD, TRD and suicidal ideation or behavior in patients with long COVID syndrome. The association between an activated immune response and the development of MDD has been previously demonstrated. Thereby, translational findings from this sample of COVID survivors with regards to the onset of depressive symptomatology early on during the SARS‐CoV‐ 2 infection and sustained peripheral inflammation is supported by previous evidence on MDD and neuroinflammation. Most studies are necessary to understand the causal mechanisms underlying this significant association and characterization of COVID survivors with immune activation and evolving affective psychopathology.

ref.mixed-citation: Xu E , Xie Y , Al‐Aly Z . Long‐term neurologic outcomes of COVID‐19. Nat Med. 2022;28(11):2406–2415. 10.1038/s41591-022-02001-z 36138154 PMC9671811
